behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT cultured (in the lab)?
questions.explanation: Aspergillus (Sabouraud), herpes (cell culture) and influenza (eggs/MDCK) are all cultured. Protozoa are diagnosed by microscopy/serology, not routine culture. (HPV is the classic 'cannot be cultured' but isn't listed.)

questions.stem: Which statement is correct?

options.A: Atypical lymphocytes are an early finding of EBV infection
options.B: Serological tests do not contribute to CMV diagnosis
options.C: Adenoviruses are related to the common cold
options.D: Plasmodia are parasites of white blood cells

questions.explanation: Atypical lymphocytes are an early finding in EBV mononucleosis. Serology DOES help CMV dx; adenovirus = pharyngoconjunctival (not simply common cold); plasmodia infect red cells, not white.

questions.stem: Which is correct?

options.A: Neutrophil count is unrelated to the course of aspergillus infection
options.B: Allergic manifestations are uncommon in aspergilloses
options.C: Pityriasis versicolor is due to the genus Candida
options.D: Cryptococcosis usually manifests as a CNS infection

questions.explanation: Cryptococcosis classically presents as CNS disease (meningitis). Neutropenia is a key risk in invasive aspergillosis; allergic manifestations (ABPA) ARE common; pityriasis versicolor is due to Malassezia, not Candida.

questions.stem: Which viruses can affect the eye?
questions.explanation: HSV (keratitis), adenovirus (pink eye/keratoconjunctivitis) and Coxsackie (acute haemorrhagic conjunctivitis) can all infect the eye → all apply.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an intestinal parasite?
questions.explanation: Leishmania is transmitted by sandfly and lives in macrophages (skin/viscera), not the gut. Ascaris, pinworm and amoeba are intestinal.

questions.stem: Which of the following causes diarrhoea?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus, Norovirus and Entamoeba histolytica all cause diarrhoea → all apply.

questions.stem: Which of the following is intracellular?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium is intracellular (hepatocytes, then RBCs). Giardia and Trichomonas are luminal; Cryptococcus is extracellular.

questions.stem: Hepatitis A virus is transmitted mainly:
questions.explanation: HAV is a naked, acid-stable picornavirus transmitted faecal-orally through the GI tract.

questions.stem: Which of the following causes congenital infections?
questions.explanation: Toxoplasma, Parvo B19 and CMV are all classic causes of congenital infection → all apply (TORCH).

questions.stem: Which virus possesses reverse transcriptase (reverse transcriptase)?
questions.explanation: HIV (retrovirus) is the canonical answer. ⚠ Note: HBV also has reverse-transcriptase activity (replicates via an RNA intermediate) — if HBV is the only listed answer elsewhere, pick HBV.

questions.stem: Epstein–Barr virus:

options.B: Causes a large decrease in lymphocytes

questions.explanation: EBV is a herpesvirus (not adeno), causes lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes (not a decrease), and is implicated in neoplasias (Burkitt, nasopharyngeal ca, Hodgkin).

questions.stem: Which of the following remains permanently in the organism?
questions.explanation: Toxoplasma (tissue cysts), CMV and VZV all establish lifelong latency → all apply.

questions.stem: Which virus is NOT transmitted sexually?
questions.explanation: Rubella spreads by respiratory droplet / vertically, not as an STD. HSV, HBV and CMV can all be sexually transmitted.

questions.stem: Which condition is NOT associated with Picorna viruses?
questions.explanation: Enteroviruses (picorna) cause pharyngitis (herpangina), myocarditis (Coxsackie B) and aseptic meningitis. Haemorrhagic cystitis is an adenovirus feature.

questions.stem: What is true about yeasts?

options.A: They have a fluffy macroscopic appearance
options.C: They are unrelated to human normal flora
options.D: They are frequently related to daily diet and living

questions.explanation: Yeasts are unicellular with moist/pasty colonies (moulds are fluffy/multicellular), and Candida IS part of normal flora. They are linked to daily diet/living (bread, beer, fermentation).

questions.stem: Which virus has RNA as its genetic material?
questions.explanation: Corona is an RNA virus. Adeno, varicella (herpes) and HPV are DNA viruses.

questions.stem: Which viruses are distinguished into dozens of serotypes?
questions.explanation: Rhinoviruses have ~100–160 serotypes → no lasting immunity, no vaccine.

questions.stem: Infection by the rabies virus:

options.B: Is due to the bite of any animal

questions.explanation: Rabies is controlled by vaccinating animals (+ post-exposure prophylaxis). It follows bites of mammals (not birds/any animal) and is almost always fatal once symptomatic.

questions.stem: Which of the following injectable vaccines is from a live attenuated microorganism?
questions.explanation: Measles (MMR) is a live-attenuated injectable vaccine. Injectable flu = inactivated; HPV & HBV = recombinant subunit.

questions.stem: What is true about amphotericin B?

options.A: Acts on the fungal cell wall
options.C: Acts on the fungal cell membrane

questions.explanation: Amphotericin B is a polyene that binds ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane and is fungicidal — not the wall, not fungistatic, not an azole.

questions.stem: Which parasite's laboratory diagnosis is done on a vaginal smear sample?
questions.explanation: Trichomonas vaginalis is diagnosed by wet-mount/vaginal smear (motile trophozoites).

questions.stem: Influenza vaccination should be done:
questions.explanation: The strains drift yearly, so the flu vaccine is given annually.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT intracellular?
questions.explanation: Cryptococcus is an extracellular encapsulated yeast. Plasmodium and Toxoplasma are intracellular.

questions.stem: For the prevention of malaria today, the following is applied:
questions.explanation: Malaria control relies on vector (mosquito) control.

questions.stem: Which virus can cause sterility in men?
questions.explanation: Mumps causes orchitis, which can lead to male sterility.

questions.stem: Which of the following does NOT affect the liver?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus is confined to the gut. Amoeba (liver abscess), Echinococcus (hydatid cyst) and EBV (hepatitis) all involve the liver.

questions.stem: Which virus is NOT potentially oncogenic?
questions.explanation: HCV, HPV and HTLV are oncogenic. CMV is not classically oncogenic.

questions.stem: Which part of a virus is mainly composed of protein?
questions.explanation: The capsid is the protein shell — mainly protein.

questions.stem: Which one belongs to the paramyxoviruses?
questions.explanation: RSV, measles and mumps are all Paramyxoviridae → all correct.

questions.stem: Which of the following remains in the body after the initial infection?
questions.explanation: Toxoplasma (cysts) and HSV (latency) persist; measles can persist as defective virus (SSPE) → the intended answer is all are correct.

questions.stem: Which laboratory term does NOT pertain to a virus?
questions.explanation: VDRL is a syphilis (Treponema) test. Monospot (EBV), p24 (HIV) and Negri bodies (rabies) are viral.

questions.stem: Nucleotide analogs are:
questions.explanation: Nucleos(t)ide analogues (e.g. acyclovir, tenofovir) are a class of antiviral drug.

questions.stem: For which virus is there NO vaccine available?
questions.explanation: CMV has no vaccine. Measles, rabies and rotavirus vaccines exist.

questions.stem: CD4 cell count is measured for the laboratory monitoring of:
questions.explanation: CD4 count is the key marker of HIV disease progression.

questions.stem: Which of the following belongs to the dermatophytes?
questions.explanation: Microsporum (with Trichophyton, Epidermophyton) is a dermatophyte. The others are not.

questions.stem: The isolation of viruses is done in:
questions.explanation: Viruses need living substrates — cell cultures, embryonated eggs, or animals.

questions.stem: All hepatitis viruses:

options.B: None of the answers are correct

questions.explanation: Not all are DNA (most are RNA), not all become chronic (HAV/HEV don't), not all are vaccine-preventable (no HCV vaccine) → none of the answers is correct.

questions.stem: The varicella virus can cause:
questions.explanation: VZV → varicella pneumonia, encephalitis and (on reactivation) herpes zoster → all correct.

questions.stem: Opportunistic mycoses:

options.A: Are due to fungi widespread in nature or colonizers
options.D: Are independent of the level of immunity

questions.explanation: Opportunistic mycoses arise from ubiquitous fungi or colonisers (Candida, Aspergillus) taking advantage of impaired immunity.

questions.stem: Mode of reproduction of viruses:

options.B: All apply, depending on the virus

questions.explanation: Viruses reproduce by assembly of separately-made components — never binary fission.

questions.stem: Which virus is transmitted by animals?
questions.explanation: West Nile (birds/mosquito), rabies (mammals) and Hantavirus (rodents) are all zoonotic → all of the above.

questions.stem: What is NOT true about Trichomonas vaginalis?

options.C: Diagnosed by trophozoites in a direct smear

questions.explanation: Trichomonas causes vaginitis, not diarrhoea. The haemorrhagic diarrhoea statement is false.

questions.stem: Polyenes:

options.B: Are also used as antibacterial agents

questions.explanation: Polyenes = amphotericin B and nystatin (bind ergosterol in the fungal membrane).

questions.stem: Myocarditis/pericarditis can be caused by which virus?
questions.explanation: Coxsackie B is the classic cause of viral myocarditis/pericarditis.

questions.stem: Which clinical sample is best for detecting poliovirus?
questions.explanation: Poliovirus (enterovirus, faecal-oral) is best detected in stool.

questions.stem: An increase in eosinophils in peripheral blood is seen in infections from:
questions.explanation: Helminth infections classically cause peripheral eosinophilia.

questions.stem: Which of the following viruses is NOT potentially oncogenic?
questions.explanation: HPV, EBV, HBV, HCV, HHV-8 and HTLV are all oncogenic → none of the above (all are oncogenic).

questions.stem: Which virus preferentially infects human erythroid progenitor cells?
questions.explanation: Parvovirus B19 targets erythroid progenitors → aplastic crisis.

questions.stem: What is true about Aspergillus?
questions.explanation: Aspergillus causes allergic reactions (ABPA, allergic antigens). It grows well and can cause severe invasive disease.

questions.stem: Which is the most common cause of congenital infection?
questions.explanation: CMV is the most common congenital infection ('owl's-eye' inclusions).

questions.stem: Which virus most commonly causes severe gastroenteritis in infants?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus is the leading cause of severe infant gastroenteritis (now reduced by vaccine).

questions.stem: A vaccine exists for:
questions.explanation: Yellow fever has a live-attenuated vaccine. No HCV vaccine; malaria vaccine wasn't routine at exam time.

questions.stem: Which virus can cause haemorrhagic cystitis?
questions.explanation: Adenovirus causes haemorrhagic cystitis (esp. in children/transplant).

questions.stem: Which of the following has NOT been proven to transmit HIV?
questions.explanation: HIV is not transmitted by mosquitoes. Blood-contaminated tools, transplants and breastfeeding can transmit it.

questions.stem: After close contact with someone with shingles, a non-immune person risks developing:
questions.explanation: Exposure to zoster in a non-immune person causes primary VZV = chickenpox (you can't 'catch' shingles).

questions.stem: What is NOT true about the measles virus?
questions.explanation: Measles has ONE serotype — 'many serotypes' is false.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an intestinal cestode (tapeworm)?
questions.explanation: Echinococcus is a cestode (tapeworm). Pinworm & Ascaris are nematodes; Giardia is a protozoan.

questions.stem: The CD4/CD8 ratio is determined for monitoring:
questions.explanation: CD4/CD8 ratio monitors HIV infection.

questions.stem: Which virus belongs to the paramyxoviruses?
questions.explanation: RSV, measles and mumps are all paramyxoviruses → all of the above.

questions.stem: Which combination of positive markers indicates recovery and immunity after infection?
questions.explanation: Resolved infection with immunity = Anti-HBs (+) & Anti-HBc (+) (HBsAg negative). Anti-HBs alone = vaccination only.

questions.stem: The flu vaccine includes:

options.A: A and B strains, usually different each year
options.B: Strains from all three types of flu
options.C: Only the strain from the most recent pandemic
options.D: A GM strain with genes from all types

questions.explanation: The seasonal vaccine contains A (H1N1, H3N2) and B strains, updated yearly.

questions.stem: Congenital rubella:

options.A: Is more severe if contracted in the 3rd trimester
options.C: Severity is inversely proportional to gestational age

questions.explanation: Damage is worst early in pregnancy — severity is inversely proportional to gestational age (worst in the 1st trimester).

questions.stem: Fungal cultures:

options.B: Do not require a combination of media
options.D: Are a diagnostic method with satisfactory sensitivity

questions.explanation: By elimination: fungi DO need special/combined media and grow at 25–30°C (not accelerated at 40°C). The intended 'true' option is that culture is a useful diagnostic method.

questions.stem: Which virus provides short-lived, ineffective immunity after infection?
questions.explanation: Rhinovirus (100+ serotypes) gives short, type-specific immunity → repeat colds. The others give lasting immunity.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT transmitted by mosquitoes?
questions.explanation: Leishmania is transmitted by sandflies, not mosquitoes.

questions.stem: Which finding indicates congenital infection?
questions.explanation: IgM does not cross the placenta, so IgM in the neonate proves the infant was itself infected (congenital).

questions.stem: Which virus does NOT affect the respiratory system?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus affects the GI tract. RSV, measles and CMV all cause respiratory disease.

questions.stem: Azidothymidine (AZT) is used to treat infection by:
questions.explanation: AZT/zidovudine is an anti-HIV reverse-transcriptase inhibitor.

questions.stem: A virus establishes itself in cells of:

options.A: Any tissue of a specific animal species
options.B: A specific tissue in any species
options.C: Specific tissues in specific animal species

questions.explanation: Tropism = specific tissues of specific species (receptor-determined).

questions.stem: Which part of a virus is mainly made of protein?
questions.explanation: The capsid is the protein shell.
